title: Presigned Browser Uploads, POST Policies, and CORS · Go Retrieval
summary: A delayed handoff reconstructs presigned browser uploads, post policies, and cors from browser, Go service, S3, CloudFront, security, billing, and stakeholder evidence, then challenges one retained misconception. Planned artifact: Extend the evidence record for least-privilege direct-upload session contract after a delayed changed case. Learners progress through prediction, explicit theory, changed-case practice, causal debugging, and evidence defense.
objectives: Create a one-object upload intent with tenant, generated key, expected size, type, checksum, expiry, status, and replay policy before signing.
Presign PUT with exact region, expiry, key, checksum, content type, encryption, and required header parity.
Use a POST policy when browser-enforced key, size, metadata, and field conditions are required and validate the completed object independently.
Preserve previously learned constraints and attach observable changed-case evidence.

steps.title: S3, CloudFront, and Go Upload Session Intent · changed case · Review Upload Session Intent
steps.instruction:
review case — A review Go file-delivery team handles case 1179 during an SDK and infrastructure canary. Build deterministic Go evidence to finalize a direct upload against intent while separating CORS from IAM authorization; keep browser execution deterministic and move AWS claims to an authorized transfer gate; then cancel halfway through and prove every body, part, and goroutine reaches a terminal state. Browser code uses pure values and state transitions only. AWS SDK packages, networks, S3, CloudFront, KMS, IAM, WAF, databases, credentials, host state, race and leak checks, load, faults, restore, billing, and production behavior require explicit authorized transfer gates. Competency probe: Create a one-object upload intent with tenant, generated key, expected size, type, checksum, expiry, status, and replay policy before signing.

Which action demonstrates Upload Session Intent without dropping earlier contracts?

Evidence target: S3, CloudFront, and Go Upload Session Intent · changed case · Review Upload Session Intent.

For S3, CloudFront, and Go Upload Session Intent · changed case · Review Upload Session Intent, keep Upload Session Intent connected to executable Go storage and delivery decision evidence.

content.title: Evidence defense
content.text: Use the case, one assumption, one observable result, and one limitation. Do not copy the competency statement: Finalize by matching intent to HEAD or event evidence, checksum, version, size, owner, scan result, and idempotent state transition.

hints: For S3, CloudFront, and Go Record what required effort · Review Direct Upload Finalization, name the stakeholder, file, object version, authorization, byte, time, concurrency, cache, cost, cleanup, and recovery boundary, Direct Upload Finalization precondition, or invariant that must hold.

At S3, CloudFront, and Go Record what required effort · Review Direct Upload Finalization, trace this concrete input and predict authorization, custody, integrity, range, cache, retry, cost, cleanup, and recovery evidence before reviewing it: A review Go file-delivery team handles case 9274 during a high-traffic evidence intake window. Build deterministic Go evidence to finalize a direct upload against intent while separating CORS from IAM authorization; record cleanup, rollback, restoration, and residual-risk ownership; then introduce a duplicate event or ambiguous response and reconcile without duplicate effect. Browser code uses pure values and state transitions only. AWS SDK packages, networks, S3, CloudFront, KMS, IAM, WAF, databases, credentials, host state, race and leak checks, load, faults, restore, billing, and production behavior require explicit authorized transfer gates. Competency probe: Finalize by matching intent to HEAD or event evidence, checksum, version, size, owner, scan result, and idempotent state transition.

For S3, CloudFront, and Go Record what required effort · Review Direct Upload Finalization, challenge the documented misconception with file, object, policy, transfer, integrity, cache, fault, cost, restore, and controlled AWS transfer evidence: The browser success event alone proves the expected object is complete and belongs to the session.
